coredata.dc:title: Combating infections from drug-resistant bacteria: Unleashing synergistic broad-spectrum antibacterial power with high-entropy MXene/CDs
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Panyong
You, Qiannan
Liu, Yulu
Miao, Huimin
Dong, Wen-Fei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: The growing resistance of bacteria to antibiotics has posed challenges in treating associated bacterial infections, while the development of multi-model antibacterial strategies could efficient sterilization to prevent drug resistance. High-entropy MXene has emerged as a promising candidate for antibacterial synergy with inherent photothermal and photodynamic properties. Herein, a high-entropy nanomaterial of MXene/CDs was synthesized to amplify oxidative stress under near-infrared laser irradiation. Well-exfoliated MXene nanosheets have proven to show an excellent photothermal effect for sterilization. The incorporation of CDs could provide photo-generated electrons for MXene nanosheets to generate ROS, meanwhile reducing the recombination of electron-hole pairs to further accelerate the generation of photo-generated electrons. The MXene/CDs material demonstrates outstanding synergistic photothermal and photodynamic effects, possesses excellent biocompatibility and successfully eliminates drug-resistant bacteria as well as inhibits biofilm formation. While attaining a remarkable killing efficiency of up to 99.99% against drug-resistant Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, it also demonstrates outstanding antibacterial effects against four additional bacterial strains. This work not only establishes a synthesis precedent for preparing high-entropy MXene materials with CDs but also provides a potential approach for addressing the issue of drug-resistant bacterial infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: High-entropy materials；carbon dots；MXene；Anti-bacteria biofilm
Photothermal and photodynamic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs, MXene and MXene/CDs
Antibacterial and Anti-Biofilm of the MXene/CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2022YFC2403500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82172077, 22201298, 82071066, and 62027825), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (Grant No. ZR2022QB077 and ZR2022QB243), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. JSSCTD202145), and the Youth In-novation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) SEM images of TiVCrMoAlC3 MAX and HF-etched multi-layer MXene. (c-e) TEM images of ultrathin MXene, CDs and MXene/CDs. (f) EDS elemental maps of MXene/CDs.

(a) Fluorescence excitation spectra and photoluminescence emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution. (b) Illustration of the color coordinates and color gamut horseshoe diagram for the emission light of CDs. (c) ζ potentials, (d) FT-IR spectra (e) XRD pattern of MXene, CDs and MXene/CDs. (f-i) XPS analysis, peak positions of C 1 s (g), O 1 s (h) and N 1 s (i) of the MXene/CDs composite.

(a) The temperature curves of MXene/CDs at different concentrations under near-infrared light Irradiation. (b) Images of corresponding temperature changes were recorded during near-infrared irradiation. (c) Photothermal heating stability of MXene/CDs for four on/off cycles.

(a) UV–Vis spectra of TMB assay. (b) Fluorescence intensity of SOSG solution in Control, CDs, MXene, and MXene/CDs. (c) ESR analysis of ·OH and (d) 1O2 generated by the control, CDs, MXene and MXene/CDs. (e)Mechanism for the enhanced yield of ROS upon NIR irradiation.

(a) Images of bacterial colonies of Rosetta and (b) antibacterial efficiency against Rosetta. (c) Images of bacterial colonies of MRSA and (d) antibacterial efficiency against MRSA. (e) Scanning electron microscopy images of Rosetta and MRSA on the different samples.

(a) Results of cytotoxicity assessment of CDs. (b) Analysis of live & dead cell staining in various treatment groups. (c) Hemolysis rate after treatment with MXene/CDs at different concentrations. (d) Analysis of Zebrafish development in different treatment groups.

The preparation and application of MXene/CDs. Overall scheme to show the main synthesis procedures of MXene/CDs and its synergistic photothermal and photodynamic applications in combating drug-resistant bacterial infections.

head.ce:title: Combating infections from drug-resistant bacteria: Unleashing synergistic broad-spectrum antibacterial power with high-entropy MXene/CDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), Suzhou 215163, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), Suzhou 215163, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing resistance of bacteria to antibiotics has posed challenges in treating associated bacterial infections, while the development of multi-model antibacterial strategies could efficient sterilization to prevent drug resistance. High-entropy MXene has emerged as a promising candidate for antibacterial synergy with inherent photothermal and photodynamic properties. Herein, a high-entropy nanomaterial of MXene/CDs was synthesized to amplify oxidative stress under near-infrared laser irradiation. Well-exfoliated MXene nanosheets have proven to show an excellent photothermal effect for sterilization. The incorporation of CDs could provide photo-generated electrons for MXene nanosheets to generate ROS, meanwhile reducing the recombination of electron-hole pairs to further accelerate the generation of photo-generated electrons. The MXene/CDs material demonstrates outstanding synergistic photothermal and photodynamic effects, possesses excellent biocompatibility and successfully eliminates drug-resistant bacteria as well as inhibits biofilm formation. While attaining a remarkable killing efficiency of up to 99.99% against drug-resistant Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, it also demonstrates outstanding antibacterial effects against four additional bacterial strains. This work not only establishes a synthesis precedent for preparing high-entropy MXene materials with CDs but also provides a potential approach for addressing the issue of drug-resistant bacterial infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections, the second leading cause of death in the world, have been recognized as important public health threats [1,2]. Antibiotics are commonly employed for the treatment of diverse bacterial infections [3], but over the past few years, these antibiotics have gradually begun to lose their effectiveness in combating bacteria [4]. The increased resistance to drugs results from the widespread use of antibiotics during the coronavirus pandemic and the protection provided by biofilms created by bacterial populations attached to surfaces and extracellular polymeric substances [5,6]. Besides, the infections caused by bacteria have been related to cancer by inducing chronic inflammatory reactions and producing metabolites [7,8]. Therefore, it’s urgent to develop new methods to solve antimicrobial resistance (AMR) infections.

High-entropy materials have attracted great attention in antibacterial with special physicochemical properties [9,10]. The synergistic effect of the cocktail may be achieved by alloying a variety of metal elements (four or more elements) into a single nanomaterial, surpassing the performance of a single metal nanoparticle [11,12]. MXene, known as a two-dimensional transition metal carbide [13], has garnered significant interest among researchers owing to its unique physicochemical properties [14], leading to widespread applications in areas such as biosensing and medicine [15–17], supercapacitors [18,19], and photocatalysis [20]. The high-entropy MXene material formed by the combination of metal elements in the 3d transition state in the periodic table of elements improves the optical absorption performance [21], which is beneficial to photothermal conversion and stimulates the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [22], thus realizing the synergistic treatment of photothermal and photodynamic effect. Carbon dots, identified as zero-dimensional materials [23], have been widely employed in areas such as bioimaging and sensing [24], drug delivery [25], optoelectronic devices [26], and information encryption [27] due to their outstanding electronic transmission capability and easily modifiable covalent characteristics [28,29]. There are some articles reporting the application of composite materials constructed with CDs and MXene. Ding et al. have prepared the composite material of CD-Ti3C2Tx-EP to provide excellent long-term corrosion-resistant performance for steels [30]. Geng et al. have applied a modification of CDs onto Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, enhancing the efficiency of ROS generation and consequently achieving effective treatment of tumors in deep tissues [31]. In a word, the introduction of carbon nanomaterials, especially those containing metal ions such as Cu and Co, into MXene layers can enhance three functionalities: 1) improve its high-entropy effectiveness; 2) functionalize the MXene surface, thereby enhancing the stability of the nanosheets; and 3) hinder the recombination of charge carriers and holes through the formation of hybrid heterojunctions, facilitating electron transfer [32,33]. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, high-entropy MXene/CDs materials have been seldom reported for use in antibacterial research.

In this work, we synthesized high-entropy antibacterial materials of MXene/CDs through a straightforward approach ( Scheme 1). The exceptional ability for photothermal conversion and remarkable photodynamic effects of MXene/CDs resulted in powerful antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, surpassing an antibacterial rate of 99.99%. In addition, it not only attains broad-spectrum antibacterial effectiveness but also demonstrates outstanding biocompatibility. Consequently, this nanocomposite material broadens the scope of potential biomedical applications for high-entropy materials, providing a potential approach to addressing drug-resistant bacterial infections.

Citric acid, guanidine hydrochloride and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (China). Copper nitrate was purchased from Beijing Innochem Co. Ltd (Beijing China). Luria-Bertani broth and Luria-Bertani agar were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were not processed or purified prior to use. TiVCrMoAlC3 powder (MAX phase, 99%, 400 mesh) was purchased from 11 Technology Co., Ltd. (Jilin, China). Lithium fluoride (LiF) was obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was obtained from Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Escherichia coli (ATCC25922), Escherichia coli (bio-82271, Rosetta, chloramphenicol resistance), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC6538), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC43300, MRSA, methicillin and penicillin resistance), Bacillus subtilis (BNCC109047) and Pseudomonas Aeruginosa (BNCC336458) were purchased from Beijing microbiological culture collection center. NIH 3T3 cells and L929 cells were purchased from cell bank of Chinese academy of sciences (Shanghai, China).
These contents were supplied in the supplementary material S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, MXene and MXene/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis method of CDs was according to our previous report [34]. 2.0 g of citric acid and 4.0 g of guanidine hydrochloride were mixed and dissolved in 40 mL of distilled water, and the clear solution was obtained by sonicating for 5 mins. And then 1.0 g of copper nitrate was added, and the pH was adjusted to 11 with concentrated NaOH solution, and the solution was heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The obtained mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the precipitate was removed to obtain the supernatant. Then, the supernatant was dialyzed in a semi-permeable membrane for 24 h, the water was changed every 6 h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and the obtained liquid was obtained solids under freeze-drying and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for next use.

The synthesis method for high-entropy MXene materials referred to the previously reported procedures [35]. Add 0.666 g of LiF to 10 mL of HCl (6 M), dissolve at 300 rpm at room temperature, add 0.5 g of TiVCrMoAlC3 (molar ratio of 1:1:1:1) to the above solution, etch at 50 °C, 300 rpm for 36 h. The etched MXene was then washed three times with water and alcohol, and the solids obtained under freeze-drying were stored in a 4 °C freezer. The single layer of MXene (2 mg) was obtained after stirring in 10 mL DMSO solution.

The CDs (5 mg/mL) were assembled on the surface of MXene (0.5 mg/mL) by vigorous stirring. And the un-assembled CDs were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 minutes after continuous stirring for 48 h. The mass change before and after calculation showed that the loading ratio of the CDs was 153.4%.

All samples were irradiated by an 808 near-infrared (NIR) laser for 5 min at different power settings (0.25, 0.5, 0.75 and 1.0 W/cm2) and the corresponding temperatures were recorded. Then, the MXene/CDs with different concentrations (0–100 μg mL−1) were exposed to detect the photothermal performance at 1.0 W/cm2. And the images were recorded every 30 s by the photothermal camera. The photothermal effects of CDs, MXene and MXene/CDs at the same concentration (100 μg mL−1) were compared under laser irradiation (1.0 W/cm2, 5 min). To detect the photo-thermal stability of MXene/CDs, the real-time temperature of samples was explored with a thermal image during four circles of heating-cooling processes.
The ROS production of the material in vivo and in vitro was evaluated. And the experimental details have been provided in the experimental section in the Supporting Information S2.

At the CHI 660E electrochemical workstation, a three-electrode system was employed to conduct cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, and transient photocurrent measurements. The platinum sheet with the sample deposition served as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl functioned as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire was used as the counter electrode. For the cyclic voltammetry (CV) test, the scan rate of 50 mV/s from 0 V to 0.6 V was set in normal mode in a solution comprising 0.1 M KCl and 10 mM K3[Fe (CN)6]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted in a solution comprising 0.1 M KCl and 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4-, covering a frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 105 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV. In a 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution, the generation of photocurrent was documented during the irradiation of near-infrared light.

The antibacterial ability of the MXene/CDs against drug-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA, ATCC 43300) and Escherichia coli (bio-82271, Rosetta) was investigated by the spread plate method. And Luria-Bertani (LB) broth and Luria-Bertani agar were used as the medium. First, the appropriate antibacterial concentration of MXene was assessed. 100 μL of the bacteria suspension (1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1) was incubated with MXene/CDs (50–200 μg/mL), followed by 5 minutes of exposure to near-infrared laser irradiation (808 nm, 1.0 W/cm2). And the group of bacteria suspension incubated with phosphate buffered solution (PBS) served as a blank group. Then, 100 μL portion of the obtained bacteria suspension from each group was uniformly spread on Luria-Bertani agar plates. After cultivation after 24 h, the plate colonies of bacteria were formed and the images of the bacterial colonies were taken by a digital camera.

The antibacterial effects were evaluated with and without laser irradiation. The experimental steps are similar to those mentioned above. Firstly, under both irradiation and non-irradiation conditions (808 nm, 1.0 W/cm2, 5 min), 100 μL of bacterial suspension (1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1) was incubated with MXene/CDs (100 μg/mL). Bacterial suspensions incubated with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were used as the control group. Subsequently, a 100 μL of each obtained bacterial suspension was evenly spread on Luria-Bertani agar plates. After incubation for 24 hours, bacterial colonies were formed on the plates, and images of the bacterial colonies were captured using a digital camera.

Besides, a fluorescence-based live/dead staining assay (Calcein-AM/PI) was used to analyze the antibacterial effect of MXene/CDs. The bacteria suspensions of all groups (E.coli, Rosetta, S.aureus, MRSA, B.subtilis and P.Aeruginosa) were obtained and incubated with Calcein-AM in dark at 37 ℃ for 20 min. Then, the bacteria suspensions were incubated with PI in dark at room temperature for 5 min. Afterward, the bacteria were washed with PBS three times and the dye solutions were removed. And fluorescence images of all samples were observed by a confocal laser scanning microscopy and all cells were stained green by calcein-AM, while the dead cells were stained red by PI. Following that, the surfaces of the glass slide were washed with PBS, and fluorescent regions were stochastically photographed by confocal scanning laser microscope.

The membrane integrity and morphology of the bacteria were observed by SEM after the antibacterial experiments. Briefly, the bacterial suspensions were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution and dehydrated with ethanol (20–100%). After that, the images of bacteria were photographed under SEM.

Crystal violet staining was employed for the quantitative analysis of the biofilm. In a 100 µL bacterial suspension, the biofilm was incubated with MXene/CDs (100 μg/mL) under laser irradiation for 5 minutes, followed by a continuous incubation for 24 hours at 37 °C. Subsequently, a 0.5% crystal violet solution was applied to stain the biofilm at the bottom of a 96-well plate for 15 minutes. The surplus crystal violet dye was then eliminated by washing the plate three times with a buffer solution. Finally, absolute ethanol was used to dissolve the biofilm stained with crystal violet，and the absorbance at OD 590 was measured to evaluate the efficiency of MXene/CDs in removing the biofilm.

We first evaluated cytocompatibility and hemolysis of MXene/CDs. Then, we investigated the effects of MXene/CDs on zebrafish embryo development. The experimental details have been provided in the experimental section in the Supporting Information S3.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were used to identify the morphology and size distribution of the MXene, CDs and MXene/CDs. The MAX phase demonstrates a block-like structure ( Fig. 1a), and following HF etching, it unveils a characteristic accordion-like arrangement (Fig. 1b). This is similar to the results observed in other studies[33]. After the addition of DMSO for intercalation, thin and transparent nanolayers were obtained (Fig. 1c). As shown in Fig. 1d, the obtained CDs exhibit a uniform spherical shape. And the mean size of copper-doped CDs, as determined by transmission electron microscopy, is 4.12 nm (Figure S1). Additionally, high-resolution imaging exposes a characteristic lattice spacing of 0.18 nm, corresponding to (100) interplanar spacing of graphite (Figure S2). In the obtained mixed MXene layers, it’s evident that the loaded CDs were uniformly dispersed on individual layers, with a lattice spacing of 0.18 nm (Figure S3). EDS results also revealed a uniform distribution of C, Cu, Cr, Ti, Mo, and V elements on the MXene layers (Fig. 1f). And the blue fluorescence at 431 nm of CDs emitted when excited at 363 nm ( Fig. 2a). And the chromaticity region corresponding to its CIE horseshoe diagram is also located in the blue area (Fig. 2b). The reduction in the fluorescence intensity of CDs after binding with MXene indicates the aggregation of CDs on the MXene layers (Figure S4). The stronger π-π interactions between the aggregated particles result in non-radiative electron transitions, leading to the quenching of fluorescence in the aggregated state of CDs[36]. Furthermore, their zeta potential shifted from −30.4 eV (CDs) and −16.2 eV (MXene) to −23.16 eV (MXene/CDs) after the coupling of carbon dots and MXene (Fig. 2c). All these results indicated the successful synthesis of the composite material MXene/CDs.

The chemical groups and microstructure of MXene, CDs and MXene/CDs were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray spectrum (XPS) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The strong representative peaks at 3334.8, 3184.4, 1620.9 and 1303.2 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of −OH/N−H, O−H, CO, and C−O−C groups, respectively (Fig. 2d). And the characteristic absorption peaks at 531.8 1014.8 cm−1 are attributed to Cu–O and Cu–N functional groups, indicating the successful doping of copper [37]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of CDs at 2θ≈12.6°, 21.7° and 30.3° indicate the crystal structure, with the peak at 26° corresponding to the (002) crystal plane [38]. And the characteristic diffraction peaks of MXene at 15.5° and CDs at 30.3° shift to 16.1° and 32.5° in MXene/CDs, confirming the successful attachment of carbon dots to the MX phase (Fig. 2e). In the XRD of MXene/CDs, distinctive diffraction peaks from both CDs and MXene were observable, suggesting the successful synthesis of the composite material of MXene/CDs. Consistent with the results of EDS, XPS also reveals the presence of elements including Cu, Cr, O, V, Ti, N, C, and Mo (Fig. 2f). In the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2g), peaks at 288.24, 285.34, and 284.74 eV are attributed to characteristic features of O–CO, C–C, and CC, respectively [39]. And the O 1 s spectrum is consistent with the FT-IR results. As shown in Fig. 2h, characteristic peaks corresponding to C–O–C, C–OH, and Cu–O are observed. In high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2i), characteristic peaks at positions 399.74 and 398.64 eV indicate the presence of pyrrolic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen[40]. Furthermore, the Cu 2p spectrum exhibits five main peaks (Figure S5), with peaks at 932.54 and 934.24 eV attributed to the Cu 2p3/2 characteristics of Cu+ and Cu2+ [41]. The peak at 943.24 eV is assigned to the satellite feature of Cu2+. Peaks at 952.14 and 954.14 eV are attributed to the Cu 2p1/2 characteristics of Cu+, providing a structural foundation for subsequent radical generation.

High-entropy materials are considered emerging energy material with an attractive photothermal conversion capability and high catalytic activity [42,43]. To evaluate the photothermal property of MXene/CDs, the temperature changes of materials were recorded by an NIR temperature camera under irradiation with different power of 808 lasers. As shown in Figure S6a, the temperature of MXene/CDs solution was raised to 60 ℃ after 5 minutes of irradiation (808 nm, 1.0 W/cm2). And the composite material of MXene/CDs displays superior photothermal conversion performance compared to the individual CDs and MXene materials (figure S6b). Then, the relationship between temperature and concentration was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3a, when the power is determined to be 1.0 W/cm2, the temperature gradually increases with concentration-dependent and the corresponding images were recorded in Fig. 3b. And photothermal properties of MXene/CDs were further confirmed by stability and conversion efficiency. The increased temperature was hardly changed with four on-off switches, indicating excellent photothermal stability of composites (Fig. 3c). Within a heating-cooling cycle, the relationship between time and temperature change was investigated (Figure S6c and d), the preeminent photothermal conversion (η) was evaluated to be 42.39%, which is higher than most particles in the same period [44–46]. The excellent photothermal properties of MXene/CDs enlightened us to explore its multiple functions.

As shown Fig. 4a, the degradation of TMB and the fluorescence intensity of SOSG (Fig. 4b) were employed to assess the generation of ·OH and 1O2 by the material. At the same illumination duration, the composite material of MXene/CDs group exhibited the maximum absorption value at 652 nm, indicating its superior capability for hydroxyl radical generation. SOSG serves as an exceptionally selective fluorescent indicator for 1O2, exhibiting negligible reactivity with hydrogen peroxide radicals. However, upon binding with singlet oxygen, it emits a green fluorescence at 525 nm similar to fluorescein. As shown in Fig. 4b, the composite material group displayed a more robust fluorescence signal in comparison to the MXene and CDs groups after exposure to NIR irradiation, which suggests a notable improvement in the capacity to generate singlet oxygen. Furthermore, the efficiency of singlet oxygen generation by the MXene/CD composite material was evaluated using DPBF as a ROS probe. With increasing exposure time, the absorption peak of DPBF at 418 nm consistently decreased, indicating its efficient capacity for ROS production (Figure S7). Moreover, the electron spin resonance was used to confirm the ability of ROS generation of the composite MXene/CDs. The 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP), and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were employed as spin traps to capture the short-lived singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) generating by MXene/CDs. The ESR spectrum displays four characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 pattern for ·OH (Fig. 4c) and three equally intense characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 pattern for 1O2 (Fig. 4d). In comparison to the MXene and CDs groups, the elevated intensity observed in the ESR characteristic spectrum indicates that the MXene/CDs composite exhibits an enhanced capability for ROS. DCFH-DA was utilized to assess the intracellular production of ROS. Following a two-hour incubation of Rosetta and MRSA with MXene/CDs, fluorescence signals were analyzed using confocal microscopy. As shown in Figure S8, lower fluorescence signals are observed in both the control group and the laser group. In contrast, the MXene/CDs group exhibits an enhanced fluorescence signal, which is significantly amplified after laser irradiation. These results confirm that intracellular ROS production can be stimulated under near-infrared laser irradiation in the composite of MXene/CDs.

The creation of photocarriers and the separation of electron-hole pairs are pivotal factors in the process of photocatalysis. In order to evaluate the efficiency of electron-hole recombination in photocatalysis, we conducted measurements of the transient photocurrent response. As shown in Figure S9a, the MXene group exhibits a negligible photogenerated current signal intensity, while the CDs group demonstrates a higher signal intensity, indicating a more effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes in the CDs group. And in the group of MXene/CDs, the maximum photocurrent signal indicates that the formation of the composite material promotes the generation of photogenerated electrons and achieves better separation efficiency of photoexcited carriers. The findings are further supported by the EIS results (Figure S9b). A smaller Nyquist circle diameter corresponds to lower charge transfer resistance, signifying enhanced efficiency in the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. The composite material of MXene/CDs demonstrates a reduced resistance value, facilitating the generation of photogenerated electrons and the separation of holes.

We developed the band structure model for MXene/CDs, investigating the mechanisms through which the electronic properties of the material could improve photodynamic effects. Conduct electrochemical impedance spectroscopy analysis on the carbon dots, and based on the Mott-Schottky equation, derive two straight lines with positive and negative slopes for different potential regions (Figure S10a). This indicates the presence of both p-type and n-type conductivity in the CDs [47]. Therefore, these carbon dots exhibit a p-n-type structure with distinct p-type and n-type domains. The absorption spectrum of CDs reveals an absorption shoulder between 400 and 500 nm, with the corresponding bandgap width determined as 1.8 eV (Figure S10b). The minimum conduction band values for CDs and MXene, assessed through cyclic voltammetry, are −0.7 eV and 0.19 eV, respectively (Figure S10c and d). Based on these results, we conducted an analysis of the mechanism responsible for the generated photodynamic effects (Fig. 4e). Under NIR irradiation, CDs are excited, and the photo-generated electrons transfer to the lower energy level of MXene conduction band, thereby generating ROS [48]. On the one hand, the p-n junction potential barrier of the p-n type carbon dots hinders the recombination of electrons and holes. On the other hand, the Schottky barrier voltage constructed by the CDs and MXene interface also impedes the recombination of electrons and holes, further promoting the generation of photo-generated electrons and consequently producing more ROS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and Anti-Biofilm of the MXene/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial efficacy of MXene/CDs at various concentrations was evaluated under NIR laser for 5 minutes against Rosetta and MRSA. As illustrated in Figure S11, MXene/CDs demonstrated a discernible antibacterial effect within the range of 50–200 μg/mL. With an increase in concentration, the bacterial colonies on the agar plate decreased, indicating a dose-dependent antibacterial effect of MXene/CDs. At a concentration of 100 μg/mL, bacterial growth was nearly inhibited. Therefore, this concentration was chosen for subsequent experiments. Next, the antibacterial effects were assessed under conditions with and without NIR irradiation ( Fig. 5). In the absence of laser irradiation, bacteria colonies exhibit significant growth on the agar plate, indicating a lower antibacterial capability of the material. Following NIR irradiation, the antibacterial efficacy was further enhanced, reaching 99.99% for both Rosetta and MRSA (Fig. 5b and d). The results obtained from SEM observations of bacterial morphology and biofilm integrity are depicted in Fig. 5e. In both the control and laser groups, bacteria displayed significant aggregation with undisturbed morphology. And in the MXene group, some bacteria exhibited partial damage. But following irradiation, there was a sharp reduction in the bacterial quantity, and their structures were completely disrupted, providing further evidence of the robust antibacterial efficacy of MXene/CDs materials. Additionally, the agar plate method was employed to validate the excellent antibacterial effects of MXene/CDs against Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Bacillus subtilis (Figure S12). After incubation with MXene/CDs, these bacteria were unable to form visibly observable colonies on the agar plates. This result highlights the broad-spectrum antibacterial activity exhibited by the composite material of MXene/CDs. Live/dead bacterial staining and crystal violet staining methods were also employed to evaluate the anti-biofilm capability of MXene/CDs. For the aforementioned six types of bacteria, after 5 minutes of light irradiation, the control group exhibited bright green fluorescence, while the MXene/CDs group showed no green fluorescence but displayed bright red fluorescence (Figure S13). This observation implies that, following incubation with MXene/CDs, a substantial number of bacteria undergo cell rupture and death, thereby affirming the material's remarkable anti-biofilm capability. Crystal violet staining further confirmed the result (Figure S14). In various bacterial categories, the MXene/CDs group exhibited the most robust anti-biofilm capability of the material after NIR irradiation. These aforementioned results illustrate that the photothermal and photodynamic effects achieved by the composite material of MXene/CDs can efficiently suppress the growth of drug-resistant bacteria and achieve broad-spectrum antibacterial effect.

The potential biological toxicity of the MXene/CDs was evaluated by WST-1 assay on NIH-3T3 and L929 cells. As shown in Fig. 6a, the cell viability was 95% after being co-incubated with the different concentration of MXene/CDs for 24 h. Compared with the control group, no obvious differences were found in the MXene/CDs groups. Moreover, the live/dead cells staining was employed to further confirm the cytocompatibility. As shown in Fig. 6b, there were not significant differences in the MXene/CDs and MXene/CDs + NIR group from those in the control group, which indicated the good biocompatibility of the material in biological environments. Subsequently, blood compatibility is an important index to assess the safety of MXene/CDs. The hemolysis test was carried out by selecting mouse red blood cells. As shown in Fig. 6c, the hemolysis rate increased from 0.79% to 3.83% with the increase concentration of MXene/CDs, all of which were far below the 5% stipulated by International Organization for Standardization (ISO). And as a kind of model organism, zebrafish has been frequently used in biomedical field to verify the toxicity of materials in vivo[49]. Newborn zebra fish eggs were incubated with MXene/CDs for 5 days. As shown in Fig. 6d, the physiological status and morphological changes of zebrafish were observed at intervals of 0, 1, 3 and 5 days. From 1–3 days, the egg membrane experienced a process from integrity to rupture. On the fifth day, compared with the blank group, the zebrafish incubated with MXene/CDs successfully hatched, and there was no change in morphology. The results indicated the good biocompatibility of the MXene/CDs and its potential application in biomedical field.

In summary, we synthesized a two-dimensional antibacterial composite nanoplatform of MXene/CDs that combines synergistic photothermal and photodynamic effects, serving as a potential therapy for combatting bacterial drug resistance. The MXene/CDs composite demonstrates excellent antibacterial effectiveness attributable to its unique physicochemical properties. Under NIR irradiation, the composite material of MXene/CDs facilitates the conversion of light energy into heat through the relaxation of hot carriers and lattice thermal vibrations, achieving an efficient photothermal conversion effect. And subsequent to the integration of carbon dots into the MXene/CDs, the composite material stimulates the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) due to the unique band structure that facilitates the generation of photoinduced electrons while inhibiting the recombination of electrons and holes. The composite material achieves physical disruption of the structure of drug-resistant bacteria, simultaneously leading to an elevation of 1O2 and ·OH within their cells, further exterminating the drug-resistant bacteria. In contrast to conventional antibiotics, our research indicates that the composite material of MXene/CDs, with its capacity to tackle bacterial drug resistance and impressive biocompatibility, exhibits substantial potential for widespread use in biomedical applications.

Huimin Miao: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Wen-Fei Dong: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Qiannan You: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yulu Liu: Software, Project administration, Formal analysis. Li Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Panyong Wang: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2022YFC2403500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82172077, 22201298, 82071066, and 62027825), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (Grant No. ZR2022QB077 and ZR2022QB243), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. JSSCTD202145), and the Youth In-novation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.113874.
